observation.summary: The three-stage transition-model adaptation pipeline (discovery via corpus statistics, formalisation via LLM-labeling, governance via meta-DKS) is proposed for Phase 11+, but the Stage 1 frequency threshold (default 5) and Stage 2 sample size (N=5) are unvalidated heuristics — what corpus size and frequency threshold actually produces statistically meaningful schema proposals is empirically unresolved.

warrant.claim: The Stage 1 frequency threshold (default 5) and Stage 2 sample size (N=5) in the transition-model adaptation pipeline must not be operationalized as governing heuristics until empirical validation establishes their statistical adequacy, because premature deployment of unvalidated thresholds risks hardening provisional gap-classifications into constitutive ontological categories within the DKS schema.
warrant.data: The observation confirms that the frequency threshold of 5 occurrences and the LLM-labeling sample size of N=5 are explicitly unvalidated heuristics — no corpus-size sensitivity analysis, no power calculation, and no false-discovery rate estimate has been produced to justify these values. Schema proposals generated under these conditions would be artifacts of the chosen thresholds, not reliable signals of emergent domain structure.
warrant.warrant: A gap classification is warranted only so long as its basis — mere absence of data — is explicitly distinguished from any stronger claim about the nature of that absence. Every liminal or uncertainty category must carry a live revisability condition specifying what evidence would trigger reclassification; absent that condition, the classification has crossed from epistemic management into ontological assertion. Consequently, thresholds used to identify candidate schema transitions must be treated as methodological scaffolding, not as substantive claims about the domain, and must be held open to independent audit until validated.
warrant.backing: The warrant against hardening provisional classifications is reinforced by the level-separation condition: any instrument certifying the adequacy of a boundary condition must be structurally heterogeneous with respect to the system it evaluates. Because the Stage 1 threshold and Stage 2 sample size are internal to the pipeline being evaluated, the pipeline cannot self-certify their statistical sufficiency without importing an external validation frame — corpus linguistics benchmarks, information-theoretic minimums, or bootstrap resampling standards — that is genealogically distinct from the pipeline's own design commitments. Without such external anchoring, confidence in the thresholds must be explicitly bounded and flagged as residually circular.
warrant.qualifier: This conservative constraint applies specifically to contexts where schema proposals generated by the pipeline are intended to propagate into Phase 11+ governance structures with binding or semi-binding authority; exploratory or clearly-labeled pilot deployments with explicit revisability conditions and no downstream ontological entrenchment may proceed at lower evidential standards provided every output is marked as provisional and routed back to empirical auditors rather than treated as validated schema additions.

argument_a.evidence: Statistical practice in corpus linguistics establishes that a minimum frequency threshold for lexical generalization typically requires log-likelihood significance testing against a baseline corpus, with thresholds of 5 raw occurrences routinely shown to be insufficient for low-entropy domains where false-positive rates are high; analogously, qualitative coding reliability research demonstrates that inter-rater agreement at N=5 labeled samples is insufficient to establish label stability, with standard practice requiring N≥20–30 samples per category for Cohen's kappa estimates to be interpretable. Neither benchmark has been applied to the current pipeline, leaving both thresholds empirically unanchored.

warrant.claim: The unvalidated heuristics in the transition-model pipeline (frequency threshold=5, sample size N=5) function as implicit ontological posits that prematurely close the schema-discovery space, and their validation requires a two-tier empirical programme that treats each threshold as a revisable pragmatic posit rather than a principled constant.
warrant.data: Stage 1 of the pipeline uses a corpus-frequency threshold of 5 occurrences to nominate transition candidates, and Stage 2 uses N=5 labeled examples to formalise them via LLM-labeling. Neither value is derived from statistical power analysis, domain-specific base-rate estimation, or prior empirical calibration against known transition taxonomies. In corpus linguistics, minimum frequency thresholds for meaningful collocation are corpus-size-dependent and typically require at least log-likelihood or mutual-information testing to establish significance; N=5 is well below the sample sizes required for inter-rater reliability estimation (typically N≥20–30) or for stabilising LLM output distributions across prompt variations.
warrant.warrant: A classification schema applied to a complex relational domain must treat liminal instances as first-class diagnostic signals rather than assignment failures, and must incorporate an explicit meta-category for schema-boundary artifacts that triggers structural revision rather than forced classification. Crucially, any threshold that governs schema entry is itself a boundary artifact: it determines which phenomena are visible to the schema and which are suppressed before evaluation begins. A threshold that is not anchored to a revisability condition—a specified class of evidence whose arrival would trigger recalibration—has crossed from methodological scaffold into ontological assertion, silently asserting that phenomena below the threshold are not schema-relevant rather than merely unobserved.
warrant.backing: The warrant is reinforced by the meta-level transparency requirement: uncertainty-management strategies must never harden provisional gap-classifications into constitutive ontological categories, and every liminal category must carry a live revisability condition. Applying this to thresholds: a frequency cutoff of 5 without a stated corpus-size scaling function is structurally equivalent to asserting that sub-threshold transitions do not exist as schema candidates—an ontological claim masquerading as a methodological convenience. Additionally, the epistemic-transparency warrant requires that the layer encoding incompleteness (the threshold as scaffold) be explicitly separated from the layer making domain claims (the schema as map), and both exposed to independent audit. The current pipeline conflates these layers by allowing the threshold to silently determine schema content.
warrant.qualifier: This argument holds under the condition that the pipeline is intended to produce generalisable, corpus-independent schema proposals rather than corpus-specific descriptive inventories. If the pipeline is scoped solely to a fixed, static corpus with stable base rates, a corpus-specific threshold calibration might partially address the concern—though inter-rater reliability and LLM output stability problems at N=5 remain independent of corpus size. The argument is also qualified by the acknowledgement that some pragmatic stopping point is unavoidable; the claim is not that heuristics are illegitimate but that they must be explicitly marked as provisional posits with stated revision triggers.

argument_b.evidence: Empirical support for threshold sensitivity in schema induction: (1) Biber et al. (1998) demonstrate that collocation thresholds in corpus linguistics require corpus-size normalisation—a threshold of 5 in a 10,000-word corpus captures fundamentally different phenomena than in a 10-million-word corpus; (2) studies on LLM label stability (Gilardi et al. 2023; Zhu et al. 2023) show that with N<10 examples, prompt-induced variance in classification outputs frequently exceeds inter-category semantic distance for fine-grained distinctions; (3) inter-rater reliability literature (Krippendorff 2004) establishes that α-reliability estimates require minimum N≈20–30 to be stable, making N=5 insufficient for formalisation confidence claims. Taken together, these sources indicate that both thresholds are set below the empirical floor required for the inferential claims the pipeline makes on their basis.

contradicts.reason: Claim mismatch: A asserts 'The Stage 1 frequency threshold (default 5) and Stage 2 sample size (N=5) in the transition-model adaptation pipeline must not be operationalized as governing heuristics until empirical validation establishes their statistical adequacy, because premature deployment of unvalidated thresholds risks hardening provisional gap-classifications into constitutive ontological categories within the DKS schema.'; B asserts 'The unvalidated heuristics in the transition-model pipeline (frequency threshold=5, sample size N=5) function as implicit ontological posits that prematurely close the schema-discovery space, and their validation requires a two-tier empirical programme that treats each threshold as a revisable pragmatic posit rather than a principled constant.'

counter.counter_claim: The attacked argument's warrant already demands explicit revisability conditions and treats thresholds as methodological scaffolding subject to independent audit, yet it provides no procedural mechanism to enforce those demands; consequently, the injunction to 'hold thresholds open' is itself inert without a concrete validation programme, meaning the attacker's two-tier empirical proposal does not contradict the warrant but rather exposes that the warrant's self-imposed conditions are unfulfilled — undermining the warrant's claim to have adequately distinguished epistemic management from ontological assertion.
counter.reason: The attacker does not deny any premise or introduce a counter-example; instead they undercut the warrant by showing that its own stated sufficiency condition (explicit revisability + independent audit) is not met by the pipeline as described. The frequency threshold=5 and N=5 operate as de facto fixed constants, so the warrant's guarantee — that maintaining revisability conditions prevents the classification from crossing into ontological assertion — loses its force. The undercutting move is that the warrant is inapplicable to the actual state of the pipeline, not that the warrant is false in the abstract.

pattern.description: A warrant that incorporates self-limiting conditions (explicit revisability, threshold audits) without embedding enforcement or procedural mechanisms creates a structural hollow at its core: the normative demand is stated but operationally inert. When an attacker supplies the missing mechanism, the attack does not contradict the warrant's content but rather colonises its unfulfilled commitments—transforming an internal adequacy claim into an external dependency. This reveals a recurrent pattern in which meta-level methodological concessions (acknowledgements of fallibility, openness to revision) function rhetorically as immunisers against critique while simultaneously generating the precise vulnerability they were meant to forestall: the concession signals epistemic humility but, absent procedural grounding, the humility is performative rather than operative. The undercutting move thus works not by falsifying a premise but by demonstrating that the warrant's self-assessment (that it has adequately distinguished epistemic management from ontological assertion) is unwarranted by its own standards.
observed: adequacy claim undermined by own criteria

revised_warrant.claim: A warrant's self-limiting conditions are epistemically operative only when paired with explicit procedural mechanisms that instantiate, not merely announce, those conditions.
revised_warrant.data: Warrants that acknowledge fallibility and revisability without specifying audit triggers, revision thresholds, enforcement agents, or escalation paths leave their normative commitments structurally hollow; adversarial or corrective inputs then colonise the unfulfilled procedural space, converting the warrant's own concessions into dependency vectors rather than integrity features.
revised_warrant.warrant: Epistemic humility expressed at the meta-level (acknowledgements of fallibility, openness to revision) acquires operational force only when it is downstream of, and constrained by, concrete procedural architecture: named revision triggers, threshold conditions that are jointly sufficient to initiate review, designated arbitration roles or mechanisms, and criteria by which a proposed revision is accepted or rejected. Absent this architecture, the humility is rhetorical—it immunises the warrant against critique while generating the vulnerability it purports to forestall. A warrant that satisfies this requirement must therefore pass a two-part internal adequacy test: (i) every self-limiting condition it states must map onto at least one procedural element that can be activated without reference to the warrant's own authority, and (ii) the warrant must explicitly distinguish between its ontological commitments and its epistemic management layer, with the latter grounded in the procedural architecture rather than in further normative assertion.
revised_warrant.backing: Argument theory (Toulmin): a warrant lacking procedural grounding for its own revisability clauses cannot satisfy the adequacy condition it sets for itself, producing a performative contradiction. Epistemology of self-governance (Pettit, List): normative self-limitation is only non-arbitrary when it is constitutionally embedded—i.e., when the limiting rules are enforced by mechanisms the rule-maker cannot unilaterally suspend. Formal systems theory: a system that asserts its own corrigibility without specifying a correction protocol is underdetermined with respect to correction; any external input that supplies the missing protocol does not contradict the system but substitutes for its missing component, creating external dependency. Security/adversarial reasoning: a gap between stated policy and operational implementation is an attack surface; the attack succeeds not by falsifying the policy but by occupying the implementation vacuum.
revised_warrant.qualifier: This revised warrant holds provided that the procedural mechanisms it requires are themselves subject to an analogous adequacy test at their own level (to prevent regress, this test terminates at mechanisms whose activation conditions are empirically observable or formally decidable without further normative warrant); the warrant does not apply to purely formal or mathematical claims where revisability is governed by proof-theoretic rather than procedural norms.
